results.possibleIdentity: William Isecke was an electronics engineer and amateur radio operator active in the New York and New Jersey area during the late 1960s and early 1970s. In the mid-1960s, he held the amateur radio callsign WB2CRJ and resided in the Bronx, New York. Professionally, he was an engineer for the Automation Machinery Company, located in Weehawken, New Jersey. His technical expertise spanned several specialized fields, including solid state circuit design, automatic controls, tape recordings, and the development of strobes. Isecke was a participant in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) program, a significant organization that paired engineers and scientists with artists to explore new media and technologies. In his later years, Isecke became a prominent resident of Teaneck, New Jersey, where he dedicated over a decade to public service. From the late 1990s through at least 2008, he served on various municipal bodies, most notably as a member of the Site Plan Review Advisory Board and the Shade Tree Advisory Board. He was also an active supporter of environmental and civic causes, appearing as a donor to the Lamont-Doherty Earth Observatory and a frequent correspondent on public interest issues.
